Motion To Strike
Off Calendar Sep-05-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearance.

RULING: There being no appearance or objection, the Court adopted its tentative ruling on DEFENDANTS COLLEEN NGUYEN, as an individual, COLLEEN NGUYEN, as an office of San Francisco College of Cosmetology, Inc., SAN FRANCISCO COLLEGE OF COSMETOLOGY, INC. and CO DUONG's Motion To Strike as follows: Defendants' motion to strike certain punitive damages allegations in the complaint is granted in part with ten days leave to amend. While the repeated requests for $5,000,000 in the complaint include both compensatory and punitive damages, the lion's share is necessarily punitive damages and thus the requests for $5,000,000 runs afoul of the spirit, if not the letter, of Civil Code 3295(e). All references to $5,000,000 are stricken and in their place plaintiffs may allege that they seek a judgment in an amount according to proof and no less than $60,000 plus prejudgment interest thereon plus punitive damages. The word "fourth" in line 26 on page 27 is also striken since punitive damages are not available on a cause of action for negligent misrepresentation. Plaintiffs adequately allege punitive damages as to their conversion claim.

The prevailing party is to submit a proposed order that repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling and complies with CRC 3.1312. Judge: Harold E. Kahn; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Not reported.

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-05-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearance.

RULING: There being no appearance or objection, the Court adopted its tentative ruling on DEFENDANTS COLLEEN NGUYEN, as an individual, COLLEEN NGUYEN, as an office of San Francisco College of Cosmetology, Inc., SAN FRANCISCO COLLEGE OF COSMETOLOGY, INC. and CO DUONG's DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT as follows: Defendants' demurrer to certain causes of action in the complaint is sustained with ten days leave to amend as to the twelfth, thirteenth and fourteenth causes of action for negligence, failure to pay wages and violation of Labor Code 510 and overruled as to all other causes of action that were the subject of the demurrer. Plaintiffs have not adequately alleged the source of any duty owing to them other than their contract with defendants, thereby rendering their negligence claim insufficient. Plaintiffs have not adequately alleged that what services they performed as employees or who was their employer, thereby rendering their wages and Labor Code claim insufficient. If they can do so in good faith, plaintiffs are granted leave to correct these deficiencies. All other causes of action challenged by defendants are adequately alleged. The first cause of action for fraud sufficiently alleges a claim for promissory fraud which is distinct from the fraud claims alleged in the third, fourth and fifth causes of action. All four fraud-based claims adequately allege each of the elements of those claims. Liberally construed, the complaint alleges that defendants engaged in a business practice that was unfair, unlawful and fraudulent within the meaning of the Unfair Competition Law, codified at Business and Professions Code 17200 et. seq. The ninth and tenth causes of action for contract breaches and the seventeenth cause of action for quasi-contract violation and quantum meruit recovery are sufficient to withstand a demurrer.

The prevailing party is to submit a proposed order that repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling and complies with CRC 3.1312. Judge: Harold E. Kahn; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Not reported.

Order To Show Cause For A Preliminary Injunction
Off Calendar Aug-03-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Elizabeth Zareh of the law offices of Zareh & Associates (415-830-3031) appears as counsel for plaintiff/moving party

Attorney, Chinh T. Vo of the law offices of Chinh T. Vo (510-836-2508) appears as counsel for plaintiff/defendant/moving/opposing party

The Order to show cause regarding preliminary injunction is argued and submitted by both sides as set forth in the transcript of the certified shorthand reporter.

Plaintiff's Application for preliminary injunction is DENIED. Court considers the two related matters: (1) the likelihood of success and (2) the balance of the relative harms. The harm to the plaintiff is all compensable in money. Court finds that the harms are much greater to Ms. Nguyen than the likelihood of success on the merits tipping in favor of the plaintiffs. When coupled with what would effectively be enforcement of a contract on a preliminary injunction motion which is very rare to do and probably not permissible in this context requires denial of the preliminary injunction motion. The prevailing party shall submit a [proposed] written order that tracks the Court's ruling, verbatim and that complies with CRC 3.1312.
